title: Dog Bloody Discharge from Nose: Causes and Emergency Guide
meta_desc: Learn about epistaxis (bloody nose) in dogs, why it is considered a high-urgency situation, and how to triage your pet's symptoms effectively.
content_html:
What is Epistaxis in Dogs?

Epistaxis is the clinical term for a bloody nose. In dogs, this discharge can range from a few drops of bright red blood to a persistent, heavy flow. It may also appear as pinkish mucus or dark, clotted discharge. Because dogs use their noses for everything, any sign of blood should be monitored closely as it indicates an underlying issue within the nasal passages or a systemic health problem.

Is It an Emergency?

Urgency Level: High. A bloody nose in a dog is often a sign of a serious condition and should be evaluated by a veterinarian as soon as possible. It becomes a critical emergency if the bleeding does not stop within five to ten minutes, if your dog is struggling to breathe, or if you notice other symptoms like pale gums, lethargy, or bruising on other parts of the body. Sudden, heavy bleeding can quickly lead to anemia or shock.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear photo or short video of the discharge can be incredibly helpful for your veterinary team. A visual record allows the vet to see the volume of blood lost, the color of the discharge (which helps distinguish between fresh bleeding and older issues), and whether the blood is coming from one nostril or both. This information is vital for narrowing down causes such as foreign objects, dental disease, or clotting disorders before you even arrive at the clinic.

faqs.question: What are the common causes of a bloody nose in dogs?
faqs.answer: Common causes include trauma, foreign bodies like grass seeds stuck in the nose, nasal tumors, dental abscesses, or systemic issues like rat poison ingestion and blood clotting disorders.

faqs.question: What should I do to help stop the bleeding?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog as calm as possible to prevent their blood pressure from rising. You can gently apply a cold compress to the bridge of the nose, but do not insert anything into the nostrils or tilt the head back.

faqs.question: Can a dental problem cause blood from the nose?
faqs.answer: Yes, because the roots of a dog's upper teeth are located very close to the nasal passages, a severe tooth root infection or abscess can break through and cause bloody or purulent discharge from the nose.
